pages.text: Engineering and transport properties of high-strength green concrete containing high volume of ultraﬁne palm oil fuel ash M.A. Megat Johari a,⇑, A.M. Zeyad a, N. Muhamad Bunnori a, K.S. Arifﬁn b a School of Civil Engineering, Universiti Sains Malaysia, Malaysia b School of Materials and Mineral Resources Engineering, Universiti Sains Malaysia, Malaysia article info Article history: Received 13 March 2011 Received in revised form 2 November 2011 Accepted 2 December 2011 Available online 30 December 2011 Keywords: Ultraﬁne POFA Efﬁcient pozzolan High-strength green concrete Engineering properties Transport properties Strength Porosity Initial surface absorption Permeability characteristics abstract The engineering and transport properties of high-strength green concrete (HSGC) containing up to 60% of ultraﬁne palm oil fuel ash (POFA) have been studied. POFA obtained from a palm-oil industry was treated via heat treatment to remove excess carbon and ground to a median particle size of about 2 lm. The ultraﬁne POFA obtained was then utilized in the production of HSGCs with POFA replacement levels of 0%, 20%, 40% and 60% by mass of ordinary Portland cement. The results show that the treatment processes undertaken results in a highly efﬁcient pozzolan. For fresh concrete, the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA tends to increase the workability of the HSGCs, and retards the setting times in particular at higher POFA contents. In the case of compressive strength, the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA reduces early age strength of the HSGCs at 1, 3 and 7 days, but enhances the strength at 28 days for all HSGCs containing POFA, where strength exceeding 95 MPa was achieved for all the POFA–HSGCs. Whereas the transport properties as assessed via porosity, initial surface absorption, rapid chloride permeability, gas permeabil- ity and water permeability tests are signiﬁcantly improved with the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA, with the HSGC containing 60% POFA exhibiting the greatest improvement at 28 days. Thus, the overall results show that the ultraﬁne POFA possesses signiﬁcant potential as an efﬁcient pozzolanic mineral admixture for the production of HSGC with promisingly superior engineering and transport properties. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Palm oil industry is one of the major agro-industries in coun- tries like Malaysia and Thailand, and this industry produces a large amount of waste in the forms of empty fruit bunches, ﬁbers and kernels. These by-products are normally used as fuel to heat up boiler for generation of electricity in palm oil factories. The ash de- rived from the process has been known as palm oil fuel ash (POFA) [1]. It has been reported that approximately 3 million tons of POFA was produced throughout Malaysia in 2007 [2]. In the case of Thailand, it was estimated that more than 100,000 tons of POFA has been produced every year and the amount increases annually [3]. Hence, the vast quantity of POFA produced may contribute to environmental problem in the future if it is not disposed of prop- erly. Realizing the potential pozzolanic property of POFA coupled with aims at reducing the potential environmental problems asso- ciated with disposing of POFA, alleviating the negative implications normally linked to cement production especially green house gasses emission and preserving the non-renewable natural re- sources for producing cement, many researchers have attempted to utilize POFA as pozzolanic m

grinding, and investigating the inﬂuence of the ultraﬁne POFA on the engineering and transport properties of HSGC containing high volume of the ultraﬁne POFA. It is part of an on-going research dealing with properties and performance of HSGC containing high volume of ultraﬁne POFA, whereby up to 60% of POFA is utilized in the concrete. 2. Materials The materials used in this study for producing the HSGCs are described in the following subsection. Explanation on the treatment processes undertaken to obtain the ultraﬁne POFA is also provided. 2.1. Materials used for concrete Ordinary Portland cement (OPC) complying the requirements of ASTM [12] was used as the main binder, whereas ultraﬁne POFA was used as a supplementary bin- der up to a replacement level of 60%. Natural river sand with a ﬁneness modulus of 3.1, speciﬁc gravity of 2.7 and water absorption of 0.62% was used as ﬁne aggregate, whereas crushed granite with a maximum size of 12.5 mm, speciﬁc gravity of 2.66, water absorption of 0.48% and bulk density of 1520 kg/m 3 was used as coarse aggre- gate. A type F polymer based superplasticiser was used to obtain high workability at low water content in order to achieve the high strength. 2.2. Treatment of POFA POFA was collected from a near-by palm oil mill located in Nibong Tebal, Pen- ang, Malaysia. It was dried in an oven at 105 ± 5 /C176C for 24 h and then sieved passing a 300 lm sieve to remove coarser particles, ﬁbers and kernels which were incom- pletely burnt during the combustion in the palm oil mill. The POFA was then ground to a sufﬁcient ﬁneness using a laboratory scale ball mill to increase the efﬁciency of the heat treatment which follows. Then, in order to remove excessive unburned car- bon which will affect the potential pozzolanic properties, the POFA was heated at 500 ± 50 /C176C for 90 min in a gas furnace. The same approach was previously utilized and reported to be effective in removing the excessive unburned carbon in POFA [13], but without the second stage grinding. Subsequently, the heat treated POFA was subjected to further grinding via the same laboratory ball mill to obtain the ultraﬁne POFA. In order to ensure the uniformity and consistency of the resulting ultraﬁne POFA, necessary measures were taken to control the treatment regimes which include the number and sizes of steel ball used in both stages of grinding, the grinding time, mass of POFA fed into the ball mill, milling speed, thickness of POFA layer in the furnace during heat treatment and the duration as well as the temperature of heat treatment. 3. Mix proportions and samples preparation HSC could be produced from a wide range of mix compositions, but for higher strength levels, in particular for 28 days strength in the range of 62–138 N/mm 2, the range of cement content in the production of HSC varies between 400 and 550 kg/m 3 [14]. Water/binder ratios in the range of 0.25–0.35 are typical, but lower values have also been used. Fine aggregates with a ﬁneness modu- lus in the range of 2.5–3.2 are preferable for HSC [15]. In order to produce HSC with 28 days strength in the rage of 62–138 MPa, the use of coarse aggregate with maximum size between 9.5 and 12.7 mm is recommended [16]. Superplasticiser dosage normally varies between 2.0% and 2.5% [17]. Nonetheless, dosage rate exceeding 3% has been successfully used in HSC containing differ- ent supplementary cementitious materials [18]. In addition, exces- sive unburned carbon in POFA has been show

64.1% at 3 days; and 100.6%, 97.7%, and 91.4% at 7 days for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. Subsequently, with prolonged water curing period of 28 days, the POFA–HSGCs exhibit greater strength than the OPC concrete, with strength value of 91.4, 98.3, 104.2, and 98.1 MPa for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respec- tively with the POFA40 exhibiting the highest 28 days strength. Hence, all of the POFA–HSGC mixes achieve greater strength than the OPC concrete at 28 days, with an increase of 7.5%, 14% and 7.3% for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. This could be attributed to the pozzolanic reaction between the POFA and Ca(OH)2, to produce secondary calcium–silicate–hydrate (C–S–H), densifying the concrete microstructure, resulting in higher strength. By utilizing a binder content of 560 kg/m 3, water/binder ratio of 0.28 and POFA replacement levels of 10%, 20% and 30% in the produc- tion of HSC, Sata et al.[9] reported 28 days compressive strength val- ues of 77.5, 81.3, 85.9 and 79.8 MPa for OPC, POFA10, POFA20 and POFA30, respectively. In a study on HSC containing POFA using binder content of 550 kg/m 3 and water/binder ratio of 0.32, Tangchirapat et al. [10] obtained 28 days strength of 58.5, 59.5, 60.9 and 58.8 MPa for OPC, POFA10, POFA20 and POFA30, respec- (a) POFA aftter sieving through 300 µm sieve (b) Ground POFA (c) Ultrafine POFA AB C AB C AB C Fig. 2. SEM micrographs with EDX analysis of POFA. M.A. Megat Johari et al. / Construction and Building Materials 30 (2012) 281–288 285

tively. Hence, the results of these earlier studies somehow portrayed the limitation of POFA when used in HSC, whereby the maximum strength obtained was 85.6 MPa at a replacement level of 20%. This could be mainly due to the coarser particle size and greater LOI of POFA used in these studies. Therefore, the ﬁndings of the present study clearly indicate that the ultraﬁne POFA with median particle size of about 2 lm and LOI of 2.53% used in the HSGC is a much more efﬁcient pozzolanic mineral admixture, which not only leads to greater strength enhancement, but also allows the POFA to be used at higher replacement levels of 60%, and yet achieving higher 28 days compressive strength than the OPC–HSC. 5.4. Porosity and initial surface absorption The test results for porosity as shown in Fig. 6 clearly demon- strate that the use of the ultraﬁne POFA reduces the porosity of the HSGC at all of the testing ages. At 3 days, the recorded porosity is 8.4%, 7.5%, 7.6% and 7.9% for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. The porosity reduces to 7.9%, 7.2%, 6.6% and 6.1% at 7 days and subsequently to 7.8%, 6.9%, 6.0% and 5.8% at 28 days for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. In addition, it is also evident that at 3 days, the increase of POFA content from 20% to 60% is to slightly increase porosity from 7.5% to 7.9%. None- theless, at 7 and 28 days, the opposite trends could be observed, i.e. the porosity reduces with increasing POFA content. The reduction in porosity for all concrete mixes from 3 to 28 days indicates that the OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60 record a reduction in poros- ity of 7%, 8%, 21% and 27%, respectively. Hence, the higher the POFA content, the greater the reduction in porosity with longer period of water curing. In the case of initial surface absorption test (ISAT), the test re- sults which was performed on 3, 7 and 28 days are shown in Fig. 7 . The results for ISAT values determined for time interval of two hours clearly show that the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA re- duces the ISAT values of the POFA–HSGC in comparison to the OPC–HSC, in particular at higher POFA content of 40% and 60%, as well as at longer curing period of 28 days. In comparison to the OPC–HSC, the POFA–HSGC experiences a reduction in ISAT val- ues of 42%, 61%, and 67% for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respec- tively. Concrete mix Slump (mm) Initial setting time (min) Final setting time (min) OPC 190 140 285 POFA20 210 230 385 POFA40 225 270 460 POFA60 230 350 555 Fig. 4. Inﬂuence of ultraﬁne POFA on compressive strength development of HSGC. Fig. 5. Relative strength of HSGC with different ultraﬁne POFA content. 286 M.A. Megat Johari et al. / Construction and Building Materials 30 (2012) 281–288

1. The inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA tends to reduce the water demand of the HSGC which leads to higher workability at con- stant water/binder ratio, with higher workability at higher POFA content. 2. The effect of the ultraﬁne POFA is to retard both the initial and ﬁnal setting times of the HSGC with greater retardation at higher POFA content. 3. The POFA–HSGC exhibits lower compressive strength at 1, 3 and 7 days especially at higher POFA contents, but the opposite trend is shown after 28 days of water curing period; whereby all POFA–HSGCs register higher compressive strength than the OPC–HSC with the POFA40 HSGC exhibiting the highest com- pressive strength of 104 MPa at 28 days. In addition, even at 60% substitution level, the POFA–HSGC exhibits higher 28 days strength than the OPC–HSC. 4. The POFA–HSGC records lower porosity, initial surface absorp- tion, rapid chloride permeability, gas permeability and water permeability in particular at higher POFA content and at longer water curing period. This could be translated into potentially superior durability performance. 5. The ultraﬁne POFA with smaller particle size, greater surface area, lower carbon content and LOI as well as high amorphous content proves to be a highly efﬁcient pozzolan which signiﬁ- cantly enhances the engineering and transport properties of the HSGC; hence, it is possible to produce HSGC utilizing high volume of ultraﬁne POFA with promisingly superior properties and durability performance. Use of palm oil fuel ash as a supplementary cementitious material for producing high-stren

are also similar with those reported in a recent study by Kroehong et al. [30], where they obtained about 70% amorphous content of the POFA using a quantitative X-RD analysis based on the Rietveld method. Hence, on the basis of similarity in the X-RD pattern and the observed phases of the POFA with those of earlier studies [13,29,30], it is expected that the POFA used in the present study should also have high amorphous content. Therefore, owing to the small particle size, large surface area, low carbon content and LOI as well as high amorphous content, all of which contribute to superior pozzolanic properties, the ultraﬁne POFA is expected to have signiﬁcant inﬂuence on the engineering and transport proper- ties of the HSGCs. 5.2. Workability and setting times The results for workability assessed by means of the slump test and setting times determined via the penetration resistance test are given in Table 4 . In the case of workability, it is clear that the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA improves the workability of the HSGC, with higher workability at higher POFA content. The ob- served increase in workability could be partly attributed to the greater binder paste volume of the HSGCs especially at higher POFA content as a result of the lower speciﬁc gravity of POFA in comparison to that of OPC. The excess paste volume in comparison to the OPC HSC could have provided better roles of coating the par- ticles of aggregates, ﬁlling the gaps between the particles of aggre- gates as well as providing lubrication for aggregate particles to move during the slump test, hence increasing the workability. This increase is despite the fact that the ultraﬁne POFA is having smaller particle size and greater surface area than OPC, as well as constant water/binder ratio and superplasticiser dosage used in all concrete mixes. Thus, this could be translated into a lower dosage of super- plasticiser to obtain constant workability. Different trend was pre- viously reported on HSC containing POFA as an increase in superplasticiser dosage was observed at higher POFA content to maintain almost constant slump [9–11]. Similarly, an increase dos- age of superplasticiser with the inclusion of POFA was reported in mortar to obtain consistent ﬂow [6]. The different trend could be due to the higher amount of LOI of POFA used in the earlier studies, which was probably contributed by the high carbon content of the POFA [6,9–11] and different type as well as dosage of superplasti- ciser utilized from that of the present study. The inﬂuence of car- bon content in POFA has been reported to reduce the efﬁciency of superplasticiser [13]. In addition, the ﬂuidity of superplasticised cement paste was observed to reduce with the inclusion of POFA, indicating the higher water demand of the cement paste contain- ing POFA, and this was attributed to the greater surface area of the highly porous nature of POFA [13]. Again this trend is very much contradicting the ﬁnding of the present study. Nonetheless, higher ﬂuidity was observed for white cement paste containing different types of superplasticisers when compared with low alkali cement and ordinary Portland cement, despite the greater ﬁneness of the white cement [31]. Similarly, higher slump of HSC concrete containing silica fume was observed when compared with the con- trol concrete with similar mix proportions, but without the silica fume [18]. Hence, for superplasticised cement paste and concrete, higher ﬁneness of the binder

absorption of the HSGC. The porosity test was performed using a vacuum saturation apparatus [22] on 55 mm diameter /C2 40 mm thick concrete core samples. In the case of initial surface absorp- tion, the test was performed on 100 mm concrete cube according the recommendations given in BS 1881: Part 208 [23]. The porosity (P) was calculated using the following equation: Pð%Þ¼ W2 /C0 W4 W2 /C0 W3 /C18/C19 /C3 100 ð1Þ where W2 is the mass of sample in saturated and surface dry condi- tion in air, W3 the mass of sample in water, and W4 is the mass of oven dry sample. 4.5. Rapid chloride permeability, gas permeability and water permeability The rapid chloride permeability test (RCPT) was performed according to the recommendations given by ASTM 1202 [24] on cylindrical concrete specimens with diameter of 100 mm and 50 mm thick using 60 V DC current for 6 h. The total charge passed (TCP) was obtained and to be related to the resistance of the sam- ple to chloride ion penetration. For the case of gas permeability, a gas permeameter similar to that developed by Cabrera and Lynsdale [25] was used to measure the gas permeability of cylindrical concrete samples of 55 mm diameter and 40 mm thick. The coefﬁcient of gas permeability was calculated using the modiﬁed Darcy’s equation (Eq. (2)) as pro- posed by Grube and Lawrence [26]: K ¼ 2lPoutQL AðP2 in /C0 P2 outÞ ð2Þ where Q is the volume ﬂow rate (m 3/s), L is sample thickness (m), Pin is pressure at inlet (bar), and A is cross-sectional area of sample (m2), l is the viscosity of the gas (Ns/m 2), and outlet pressure, Pout = 1 bar. Water permeability was performed using the same permeabil- ity cell used for the determination of gas permeability, on similar cylindrical concrete samples of 55 mm diameter and 40 mm thick. The procedure involved water penetrating the top surface and ﬂowing through the sample under an applied pressure head of 2–4 bars. The coefﬁcient of water permeability, Kw, was calculated using the following equation [27]: KW ¼ d 2 m 2Th ð3Þ where d is the depth of water penetration (m), T the time of pene- tration (s), h the applied pressure (m) and m is the total porosity (fraction). Porosity, m, was calculated using the following equation: m ¼ m Ad q ð4Þ where m is the gain in mass (kg); A the cross-sectional area of spec- imen (m 2), q is the density of water (1000 kg/m 3). 5. Results and discussion 5.1. Physical properties and chemical compositions of POFA The changes in the physical properties and chemical composi- tions of the POFA due to the treatment processes are shown in Tables 1 and 2 , respectively. The particle size distribution of the POFA at different stages of the treatment process and that of the OPC are shown in Fig. 1. The ﬁnal ultraﬁne POFA recorded a median particle size of 2.06 lm with a speciﬁc surface area of 1.775 m 2/g. Hence, in comparison to the original POFA that was sieved passing 300 lm sieve, the ultraﬁne POFA records greater than 80% reduction in median particle size and more than 300% increase in surface area. In addition, the recorded median particle size is about 70% smaller than that of the OPC, while the surface area is about 120% greater than that of the OPC used in the study. Thus, the ultraﬁne POFA has signiﬁcantly smaller particle size and higher surface area than those of the original POFA and the OPC. It should be emphasized that the surface area for the POFA and OPC was measured using a laser diffraction technique, hence it may not be appr

surface absorption could be attributed to the more reﬁned micro- structure of the POFA–HSGC resulting from the pozzolanic reaction of the ultraﬁne POFA, in particular at higher POFA content and longer water curing period. 5.5. Rapid chloride permeability, gas permeability and water permeability The RCPT was used to assess the potential chloride resistant per- formance of the HSGC and the results are shown inFig. 8. It is obvious that in comparison to the OPC–HSC, the TCP values generally in- crease for the POFA–HSGCs at 3 days. Nonetheless, at longer water curing periods of 7 and 28 days, the TCP values of the POFA–HSGCs signiﬁcantly reduce in comparison to the OPC–HSC. In fact, at 28 days, there is a clear and consistent trend that the TCP values re- duce with increasing POFA content, with TCP values of 2038, 648, 363 and 327 coulombs for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. This shows that the POFA–HSGCs exhibit a reduction in TCP of 68%, 82% and 84%, respectively for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60. The signiﬁcant reduction in TCP could potentially be trans- lated into superior chloride resistant performance of the POFA– HSGC. Similar trend was previously observed by Chindaprasirt et al. [6] in particular for concrete using ﬁner POFA. For the case of gas permeability, the test results obtained in the form of coefﬁcient of gas permeability are shown in Fig. 9. The ﬁgure clearly shows that the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA tremendously reduces the coefﬁcient of gas permeability of the HSGC at all testing ages. At 3 days, the OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60 record a coefﬁcient of gas permeability of 6.58, 4.36, 2.89, 1.55 /C2 10 /C0 17 m2,r e s p e c t i v e l y , while at 28 days the coefﬁcient of gas permeability reduces to 3.67, 1.48, 1.14 and 0.88 /C2 10/C0 17 m2, respectively. Hence, in comparison to the OPC–HSC, the effect of the ultraﬁne POFA is to consistently reduce the 28 days coefﬁcient of gas permeability of the POFA-HSGCs by 60%, 69% and 76% for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. Similarly, as in the case of gas permeability, the inclusion of the ultraﬁne POFA signiﬁcantly reduces the water permeability of the HSGCs ( Fig. 10 ) especially at longer water curing of 28 days. After 3 days of curing, a coefﬁcient of water permeability of 5.05, 3.42, 4.58 and 4.92 /C2 10/C0 11 m/s was recorded for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively. This shows that the ultraﬁne POFA low- ers the coefﬁcient of water permeability albeit the seeming trend of higher coefﬁcient of water permeability at higher POFA content which could be attributed to dilution effect. Nonetheless, after pro- longed period of water curing of 28 days, the recorded coefﬁcient of water permeability is 2.16, 1.77, 1.65 and 1.45 /C2 10/C0 11 m/s for OPC, POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively, indicating a con- sistent reduction in the coefﬁcient of water permeability with higher POFA content. In comparison to the OPC–HSC, the POFA– HSGCs exhibit a reduction in coefﬁcient of water permeability of 18%, 24% and 33% for POFA20, POFA40 and POFA60, respectively at 28 days. Earlier, Tangchirapat et al. [10] reported that HSC con- taining 20% POFA provided the lowest coefﬁcient of water perme- ability, and the coefﬁcient of water permeability increased at higher POFA content of 30%, which is contradicting the ﬁnding of the present study. The different trend could be due to the poten- tially more reactive nature of the ultraﬁne POFA used in the pres- ent study,
